title: Does Going Easy on Distressed Banks Help the Macroeconomy?

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of New York staff report (SR 823) by Sean Hundtofte examines the real economic effects of regulatory forbearance during the U.S. savings and loan crisis, using historical variation in deposit insurance and geographic variation in principal supervisory agents as instruments. The evidence suggests that forbearance policies induced a real estate boom from 1982-89, followed by a larger bust in both real estate values and real GDP.
key_points: Study uses historical variation in thrift deposit insurance and geographic variation in principal supervisory agents (PSA) as instrumental variables
Findings indicate regulatory forbearance created a policy-induced real estate boom during 1982-89
The subsequent bust in real estate and real GDP was larger than the initial boom
The relationship is not explained by ex ante state size, industry exposure, or systematic cyclicality
Study focuses on the U.S. savings and loan crisis as the empirical context
why_it_matters: This historical research provides empirical evidence that regulatory forbearance during banking crises may produce larger economic losses in the bust phase. For current policymakers, this offers a data-driven perspective on the tradeoffs of allowing distressed banks to continue operating versus prompt corrective action.
macro_context: The study links regulatory forbearance to real GDP outcomes, finding that the post-boom bust phase produces larger economic contractions than the initial boom, suggesting forbearance policies have asymmetric macro costs.
market_structure_context: The research examines bank regulatory structure and supervisory frameworks, specifically how differences in deposit insurance regimes and supervisory agent assignments affected the degree of forbearance across states during the savings and loan crisis.

issues_found: Source published October 2017 (revised January 2018) — approximately 8 years old, well beyond the 90-day threshold. However, per guidelines, may still be accepted as a key research finding with archival value.

Full content available only in PDF; crawl extracted limited metadata. Key quantitative findings and detailed methodology were not captured in extraction, though the summary accurately reflects available metadata.
